paragraphs.body: The thought that music is good for the soul is not new, but the degree to which it can create positive impacts on humans is something that I have often thought about. In the article, “How Music Helps People Heal: The Therapeutic Power of Music”, the author Paul Pellinger explores music from this unconventional angle and describes the physical, emotional, and mental benefits that humans can derive from music. The author presents this idea in several interesting ways, many of which resonated with me. First the author expresses how music can help with stress reduction. Listening to music you enjoy can stimulate the release of stress reducing hormones which can help reduce anxiety, lower blood pressure, and create an overall sense of relaxation. The author then goes into discussing the power of melody in music and how harmonies and melodies can bring forth specific emotions and provide comfort. These melodies can tap into your emotional expression and help individuals convey their feelings when word alone may not be enough. The author also acknowledges how music can distract from negative thoughts. For instance engaging with music can redirect attention off obsessive and distressing thoughts. Overall the author really supports the idea that music has a profound therapeutic power whether you are facing stress, pain, or mental health challenges.

annotations.context_left: hormones which can help reduce anxiety, lower blood pressure, and create an overall sense of

paragraphs.body: Personally, I've always felt a strong connection to music and have recently begun to realize that it is a critical part of my life. A song can change my mood and alter my thought process in ways that other things cannot. Like with any art form however, relationships to music are very personal. The types of music that we listen to, how we listen, and the effects that the music has on us vary in both type and magnitude. I believe that the way that I interact with music is special and may also be a good tool to help others. Everyone engages with music in their own distinctive way and Paul Pellinger highlights this by mentioning, “Certified music therapists work with individuals of all ages and backgrounds, customizing treatment plans to address specific needs and goals. Sessions may involve listening to music, playing instruments, songwriting, or even group activities.” This illustrates the versatility of music as a therapeutic tool. For many, including myself, music not only serves as therapy but also sparks a deep personal connection.

paragraphs.body: Music to me has always been a big part of my life and certain songs and artists can provide me with this feeling of comfort and can act like a form of therapy. My personal connection to music stems from my parents. From a very young age my parents introduced me to many different types of music and genres. After certain songs were being constantly repeated throughout my childhood, these songs only make me think of home and family when they are played. For instance, a song that my parents played quite often throughout my childhood was “Three Little Birds” by Bob Marley. Now whenever I hear this song I am immediately transported back to those moments in our Chester Springs home, where my family and I would dance around the living room together. I find this song extra comforting because of the smooth, fun, and reassuring lyrics and all the memories behind it. For me music is more than just sounds; it's a bridge to my family. It reminds me of all the love and support my family gives me and no matter what I am going through I always have them.

paragraphs.body: My family has always done so much for me and has gotten me through so much in my life. They always provide me with support and love. My parents are the reason I am where I am today, they provided me with the tools necessary for succeeding in life. So when the time came for me to leave home for college, it was incredibly difficult. The thought of leaving behind the family I’ve always lived with, the ones who have always been just a few steps away, was hard to grasp. The idea that they would now be four hours away seemed impossible to adjust to. It was very challenging the first few weeks as I tried to adjust to them not being so close by. But Then I found the music. It became a lifeline. The music that reminded me of my family and all the love and comfort they provide me. This music then became my bridge between home and my new life at college. Whenever I am feeling down or missing my family, these familiar songs offer a similar comfort I would get from them. I often find myself playing “Three Little Birds” on repeat. When I listen to it, I no longer feel 4 hours away, I feel like I'm back in my Chester Springs home dancing around with my family. I’m reminded of happier moments associated with that song. In the song the main line is “Don't worry about a thing, ‘Cause every little thing, Gonna be alright” and this resonates with me now more than ever. Those words that were at one point just fun lyrics now serve as a reminder that no matter the distance from my family, everything will be alright. They will always be there for me and supporting me.

paragraphs.body: In this new chapter of my life, music has become my anchor, a bridge connecting me to home and a reminder of the relationships that support me. It helps me navigate the challenges of adjusting to college life, offering support and familiarity in the middle of this change. When I find myself feeling overwhelmed or homesick, I can turn to these familiar tunes, and they transport me back to moments filled with love and laughter. As I mature and begin to experience more of life, I am comforted to know that I will always have music. People should explore their own personal relationship with music and work to understand its healing powers. It truly is good for the soul but can be even better if you dig in to understand your own personal connection.
